text.title: Writing Constitutions: Two-Mode Workflow
text.summary: Procedures for creating constitution.md in two modes: analyzing an existing codebase vs. Socratic dialogue for new projects.

list.item: Step 1 (Detect): read directory layout, CLAUDE.md, README.md, config files (pyproject.toml, package.json, .eslintrc)
Step 2 (Analyze): identify tech stack, architecture patterns, naming conventions, linter/formatter setup, testing configuration

Step 3 (Present Findings): "Analysis: 1. Tech: Python 3.11, Django 4.2, PostgreSQL. 2. Architecture: layered (views→services→models). 3. Standards: black + isort + flake8. Does this match? What to change?"
Step 4 (Draft and Review): incorporate feedback, write constitution.md

rule.statement: In Mode 1, let codebase analysis lead; do not write standards from memory or training data.
rule.rationale: Agents copy patterns from existing code without checking if those patterns are intentional standards or technical debt. Present findings to the team before committing them as standards. What looks like a convention may be an accident.

list.item: Step 1 (Vision): "Tell me about the project. What problem does it solve?" Apply Five Whys to reach the real need.
Step 2 (Tech Choices): present A/B/C with pros/cons for backend, database, frontend framework
Step 3 (Architecture): discuss monolith vs microservices, REST vs GraphQL, ORM vs raw SQL — with trade-offs for this specific project
Step 4 (Standards): linter, formatter, type hints, testing coverage, CI/CD, git conventions
Step 6 (Review): validate with team before committing
Create .aidocs/ directory structure: backlog/, todo/, in-progress/, done/
Create CLAUDE.md navigation hub in project folder

antipattern.description: Copying constitution sections from a different project or from training data without validating against the actual codebase.
antipattern.reason: Constitution copied without validation becomes "aspirational" rather than descriptive. When executor agents follow the constitution but the codebase contradicts it, they produce inconsistent code that fails review.

antipattern.description: Over-engineering the constitution for a new project by specifying microservices, message queues, and multi-region deployment from day one.
antipattern.reason: Premature architectural complexity locks in decisions that should emerge from actual usage. Start with the simplest architecture that could work; the constitution can be updated when complexity is justified.
